CAMP MATHER APPROVAL OF FEE INCREASES AND CAMPERSHIP PROGRAM Discussion and possible action to approve a Camp Mather fee increase effective January 1998. Ernie Prindle presented this item to the Commission. He stated that the last fee increase was for the 1995 season. The fee increase would address various maintenance and repair needs at the camp. All money would go back into Camp Mather. The fee increase would generate about $100,000. The Department recommended a 10% increase. In addition, the Department also recommended a tent site increase equal to half the cost of a comparable cabin. This tent site fee increase would result in a mean increase of $ 1 50.00 per family per week. Third, the day use fee which applies to non-registered guests and visitors of Camp Mather would be increased to $10.00 for adults and $5.00 for children. The Department also recommended a Camp Mather Campership Program. Camperships would be offered at random to very low-income families, as defined by the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development. This program would apply to San Francisco residents that are not able to attend Camp Mather due to financial limitations. Information on camperships would not be a part of the regular application brochure. The information would be furnished to recreation centers and random drawings of eligible families would be held prior to the camp season. Staff recommended approval of this item. On motion of Commissioner Salinas and seconded by Commissioner Flunder the following resolution was adopted: RES. NO. 9801-009 RESOLVED, that this Commission does approve a Camp Mather fee increase effective January 1998. This item was passed unanimously. CAMP MATHER APPROVAL OF POLICY REVISIONS Discussion and possible action to approve a request for Camp Mather reservations policy revisions. Ernie Prindle presented this item to the Commission. He stated that with the increased popularity of Camp Mather, applicants have found securing a reservation at camp to be challenging. As a result, some applicants eschewed the current process through, submitting multiple applications, reducing days on a reservation and making late payments. To address these issues the Department recommended revising some policies.

REQUIRED RESPONSES HRC - 60 days Office of Contract Administration - 60 days Office of the Controller - 60 days Board of Supervisors - 90 days 14 A Report of the 2002-2003 Civil Grand Jury For the City and County of San Francisco SHARING CAMP MATHER, SAN FRANCISCO'S SECRET JEWEL, WITH ALL SAN FRANCISCANS Released June 2003 Reports of the Civil Grand Jury do not identify the individuals interviewed, pursuant to California Penal Code sec. 929. The California Legislature intended this provision to encourage full candor and cooperation by City and

for the officer or agency head to be prepared to discuss it (not exceeding six months from the release of this Report); or (4) the recommendation will not be implemented because it is not warranted or reasonable, with an explanation of why that is. (Cal. Penal Code, sees. 933, 933.05.) SHARING CAMP MATHER, SAN FRANCISCO'S SECRET JEWEL, WITH ALL SAN FRANCISCANS Summary of Recommendations 1 . Improve publicity and accessibility to Camp Mather. 2. Study and improve the usage of the Camp Mather facilities and grounds. OVERVIEW Camp Mather is a family camp located in Tuolumne County near the north entrance to Yosemite National Park. Located approximately 1 80 miles east of San Francisco at an elevation of 4,520 feet, Camp Mather is operated for San Francisco residents and their families by the San Francisco Recreation and Park Department (RPD). It has 90 rustic cabins and 20 tent sites, each able to accommodate up to six people, on approximately 400 acres. Full board, a camp store, and several recreational programs are provided for the campers. During a 12-week camping season, two weeks of which are reserved for seniors, approximately 6,000 persons, in 1 , 1 00 families, take advantage of the Camp Mather facilities. The camp can accommodate 529 people at each session. The number of applications for cabins and tent sites exceeds the available spaces. The privately run Strawberry Music Festival, draws another 1 0,000 people in total to Camp Mather on the Memorial Day and Labor Day weekends. Camp Mather is financially self-sufficient and. in past years, has contributed in excess of $300,000 annually to the RPD general fund. Camping fees and revenue from SMF and other concessions exceed the expense of operating Camp Mather. In a time of budget crisis, Camp Mather could produce additional revenue. Camp Mather was described several years ago in the press as "the city's most carefully kept secret," one that most San Franciscans don't know exists. The location, lack of publicity, a complex application process, and failure to provide information about Camp Mather in languages other than English limit access to Camp Mather for many San Francisco residents.
